title: How to Implement Progressive Delivery with Feature Flags
description: A step-by-step checklist for implementing progressive delivery using feature flags, canary releases, and gradual rollouts so you can ship to production without breaking things.

items.title: Pick a feature flag system and install it
items.description: You need a central place to manage flags before you can do anything else. Self-hosted options like Unleash or Flagsmith give you full control. Managed services like LaunchDarkly or Split handle the infrastructure for you. Pick one and wire it into your app.

codeBlocks.label: Deploy Unleash with Docker Compose
codeBlocks.code:
cat > docker-compose.yml << 'EOF'
version: '3.8'
services:
  unleash:
    image: unleashorg/unleash-server:latest
    ports:
      - "4242:4242"
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: postgres://unleash:password@db:5432/unleash
      DATABASE_SSL: "false"
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
  db:
    image: postgres:15
    environment:
      POSTGRES_USER: unleash
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: password
      POSTGRES_DB: unleash
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U unleash"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 10
EOF

docker compose up -d

codeBlocks.label: Install the SDK in your app (Node.js example)
codeBlocks.code:
npm install unleash-client

# Or for LaunchDarkly:
npm install @launchdarkly/node-server-sdk

# Or for open-source Flagsmith:
npm install flagsmith-nodejs

items.title: Initialize the flag client at app startup
items.description: The flag client needs to connect early so flags are ready before your first request hits. Initialize it once at startup and reuse the instance. Never create a new client per request - that will hammer your flag service and add latency.

codeBlocks.label: Initialize Unleash client (Python)
Pass flag config via environment variables
codeBlocks.code:
# kubernetes deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-service
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: my-service
          env:
            - name: UNLEASH_URL
              value: "https://flags.internal.example.com/api"
            - name: UNLEASH_API_KEY
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: unleash-credentials
                  key: api-key

items.title: Set a naming convention for flags
items.description: Without a naming convention, you end up with flags like 'test_flag_2', 'johns-experiment', and 'new_thing_v3' scattered everywhere. Agree on a pattern upfront. Include the team, type, and purpose. This makes it easy to search, audit, and clean up later.

codeBlocks.label: Flag naming convention examples
codeBlocks.code:
# Pattern: <type>.<team>.<feature>
# Types: release, experiment, ops, permission

# Examples:
release.checkout.new-payment-flow    # shipping a new feature
experiment.growth.signup-redesign    # A/B test
ops.platform.enable-redis-cache      # operational toggle
permission.billing.enterprise-sso    # entitlement flag

codeBlocks.label: Flag metadata template
codeBlocks.code:
{
  "name": "release.checkout.new-payment-flow",
  "description": "New Stripe-based payment flow replacing Braintree",
  "owner": "checkout-team",
  "created": "2026-03-28",
  "expected_removal": "2026-05-01",
  "jira_ticket": "CHECKOUT-1234",
  "type": "release"
}

items.title: Implement percentage-based rollouts
items.description: Flipping a flag for 100% of users on day one defeats the purpose. Start with 1-5% of traffic and increase gradually. Use consistent hashing on user ID so the same user always sees the same version - otherwise you get weird behavior where features appear and disappear between page loads.

codeBlocks.label: Percentage rollout with sticky assignment
codeBlocks.code:
import hashlib

def is_feature_enabled(feature_name: str, user_id: str, percentage: int) -> bool:
    """Sticky percentage rollout based on user ID hash."""
    hash_input = f"{feature_name}:{user_id}"
    hash_value = int(hashlib.sha256(hash_input.encode()).hexdigest(), 16)
    bucket = hash_value % 100
    return bucket < percentage

# Rollout schedule:
# Day 1: 1% (internal users + small slice)
# Day 2: 5%
# Day 3: 25%
# Day 5: 50%
# Day 7: 100%

if is_feature_enabled("new-search", request.user.id, percentage=5):
    return new_search_handler(request)
else:
    return old_search_handler(request)

codeBlocks.label: Unleash gradual rollout strategy
codeBlocks.code:
# Unleash API - create a gradual rollout
# POST /api/admin/projects/default/features
name: release.search.new-algorithm
enabled: true
strategies:
  - name: flexibleRollout
    parameters:
      rollout: "5"          # start at 5%
      stickiness: "userId"  # consistent per user
      groupId: "new-search" # rollout group

items.title: Set up canary deployments in your infrastructure
items.description: Feature flags control behavior inside your app. Canary deployments control which version of your app serves traffic. Use both together: deploy the new version to a canary pod, route a small percentage of traffic to it, and watch for errors. If something breaks, the blast radius is tiny.

codeBlocks.label: Kubernetes canary deployment with Argo Rollouts
codeBlocks.code:
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Rollout
metadata:
  name: my-service
spec:
  replicas: 10
  strategy:
    canary:
      steps:
        - setWeight: 5      # 5% traffic to canary
        - pause: {duration: 10m}
        - setWeight: 25
        - pause: {duration: 10m}
        - setWeight: 50
        - pause: {duration: 10m}
        - setWeight: 100
      canaryMetadata:
        labels:
          role: canary
      stableMetadata:
        labels:
          role: stable
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: my-service
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: my-service
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: my-service
          image: my-service:v2.1.0

codeBlocks.label: Monitor canary with Argo Rollouts CLI
codeBlocks.code:
# Install the kubectl plugin
kubectl argo rollouts get rollout my-service --watch

# Promote canary to next step manually:
kubectl argo rollouts promote my-service

# Something wrong? Roll back immediately:
kubectl argo rollouts abort my-service

items.title: Add automated rollback triggers
items.description: Manual rollbacks depend on someone watching a dashboard at 2am. Automated rollbacks don't. Connect your canary deployment to your metrics pipeline and define clear failure thresholds. If error rate jumps or latency spikes, the rollout stops and reverts automatically.

codeBlocks.label: Argo Rollouts with Prometheus analysis
codeBlocks.code:
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: AnalysisTemplate
metadata:
  name: canary-success-rate
spec:
  metrics:
    - name: error-rate
      interval: 60s
      failureLimit: 3
      provider:
        prometheus:
          address: http://prometheus:9090
          query: |
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{
              status=~"5..",
              app="my-service",
              role="canary"
            }[5m])) /
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{
              app="my-service",
              role="canary"
            }[5m])) * 100
      successCondition: result[0] < 1.0   # fail if error rate > 1%
    - name: latency-p99
      interval: 60s
      failureLimit: 3
      provider:
        prometheus:
          address: http://prometheus:9090
          query: |
            histogram_quantile(0.99,
              sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{
                app="my-service",
                role="canary"
              }[5m])) by (le)
            )
      successCondition: result[0] < 0.5   # fail if p99 > 500ms

codeBlocks.label: Wire analysis into rollout steps
codeBlocks.code:
# Add to the canary strategy in your Rollout spec:
strategy:
  canary:
    steps:
      - setWeight: 5
      - pause: {duration: 5m}
      - analysis:
          templates:
            - templateName: canary-success-rate
      - setWeight: 25
      - pause: {duration: 10m}
      - analysis:
          templates:
            - templateName: canary-success-rate
      - setWeight: 100

items.title: Instrument feature flag metrics and logging
items.description: When something goes wrong in production, the first question is always 'what changed?' If you don't log which flags are active for each request, you're debugging blind. Emit metrics for every flag evaluation so you can correlate flag changes with error spikes in your dashboards.

codeBlocks.label: Log flag evaluations with structured logging
codeBlocks.code:
import structlog
from prometheus_client import Counter

logger = structlog.get_logger()

flag_evaluation = Counter(
    "feature_flag_evaluation_total",
    "Number of feature flag evaluations",
    ["flag_name", "result"]
)

def check_flag(flag_name: str, user_id: str) -> bool:
    result = client.is_enabled(flag_name, {"userId": user_id})

    flag_evaluation.labels(
        flag_name=flag_name,
        result=str(result)
    ).inc()

    logger.info(
        "flag_evaluated",
        flag=flag_name,
        user_id=user_id,
        enabled=result
    )
    return result

codeBlocks.label: Grafana dashboard query for flag evaluations
codeBlocks.code:
# Prometheus query to track rollout progress
# Shows % of evaluations returning true per flag
sum(rate(feature_flag_evaluation_total{result="True"}[5m])) by (flag_name)
/
sum(rate(feature_flag_evaluation_total[5m])) by (flag_name)
* 100

items.title: Create kill switches for new features
items.description: A kill switch is a flag that lets you turn off a feature instantly without a deployment. Every new feature that touches critical paths (payments, auth, data pipelines) should have one. The difference between a 5-minute incident and a 30-minute incident is often just a kill switch.

codeBlocks.label: Kill switch pattern

items.title: Implement user targeting for internal testing
items.description: Before rolling out to real users, test with your own team first. Target by email domain, user ID list, or custom attributes. This catches bugs that only show up with real infrastructure (not just local dev) without risking customer-facing issues.

codeBlocks.label: Target internal users first
Unleash targeting strategy

items.title: Wire feature flags into your CI/CD pipeline
items.description: Your pipeline should know about flags. Run tests with flags both on and off to catch issues in both code paths. And when you deploy, automatically update flag states instead of doing it by hand in a dashboard.

codeBlocks.label: GitHub Actions - test both flag states
codeBlocks.code:
name: Test with feature flags
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  test-flags-off:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm test
        env:
          FEATURE_NEW_CHECKOUT: "false"
          FEATURE_NEW_SEARCH: "false"

  test-flags-on:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm test
        env:
          FEATURE_NEW_CHECKOUT: "true"
          FEATURE_NEW_SEARCH: "true"

codeBlocks.label: Update flag percentage after successful deploy
codeBlocks.code:
#!/bin/bash
# post-deploy.sh - bump rollout after deploy succeeds
FLAG_NAME="release.checkout.new-payment-flow"
NEW_PERCENTAGE=${1:-5}

curl -s -X PATCH \
  "${UNLEASH_URL}/api/admin/projects/default/features/${FLAG_NAME}/strategies" \
  -H "Authorization: ${UNLEASH_API_KEY}" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"parameters": {"rollout": "'${NEW_PERCENTAGE}'"}}'

echo "Rolled out ${FLAG_NAME} to ${NEW_PERCENTAGE}%"

items.title: Set up alerts that are flag-aware
items.description: Standard alerts fire based on overall error rates. But if you just turned on a flag for 5% of users, a small error spike might not trigger a global alert. Create alerts that compare metrics between flag-on and flag-off cohorts. That way you catch regressions even at low rollout percentages.

codeBlocks.label: Prometheus alert comparing canary vs stable
codeBlocks.code:
groups:
  - name: progressive-delivery
    rules:
      - alert: CanaryErrorRateHigh
        expr: |
          (
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5..", role="canary"}[5m]))
            / sum(rate(http_requests_total{role="canary"}[5m]))
          ) > 2 * (
            sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5..", role="stable"}[5m]))
            / sum(rate(http_requests_total{role="stable"}[5m]))
          )
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "Canary error rate is 2x higher than stable"
          description: "Canary: {{ $value }}% errors vs stable baseline"
          runbook: "https://wiki.internal/runbooks/canary-rollback"

items.title: Handle flag service outages with safe defaults
items.description: Your flag service will go down at some point. When it does, your app needs to keep working. Set safe defaults for every flag - usually 'off' for new features and 'on' for existing behavior. Cache the last known flag state locally so a flag service blip does not cascade into a full outage.

codeBlocks.label: Local cache with fallback defaults

items.title: Schedule regular flag cleanup
items.description: Stale flags are tech debt that compounds fast. A codebase with 200 flags where half are permanently on is harder to reason about and test than one with 30 active flags. Set an expiration date when you create a flag. Run a monthly audit. Remove flags that have been at 100% for more than two weeks.

codeBlocks.label: Find stale flags with the Unleash API
codeBlocks.code:
#!/bin/bash
# find-stale-flags.sh - list flags that are fully rolled out
STALE_DAYS=14

curl -s "${UNLEASH_URL}/api/admin/features" \
  -H "Authorization: ${UNLEASH_API_KEY}" | \
  jq --arg days "$STALE_DAYS" '[
    .features[] |
    select(.enabled == true) |
    select(.strategies[0].parameters.rollout == "100") |
    select(
      (now - (.createdAt | fromdateiso8601)) > ($days | tonumber * 86400)
    ) |
    {name, createdAt, type}
  ]'

# Example output:
# [
#   {"name": "release.search.new-algorithm", "createdAt": "2026-02-10", "type": "release"},
#   {"name": "release.checkout.redesign", "createdAt": "2026-01-15", "type": "release"}
# ]

codeBlocks.label: Find dead flag references in code
codeBlocks.code:
# Search for flag names in your codebase
# If a flag is archived but still referenced, it needs cleanup
grep -rn "release\.checkout\.redesign" src/ --include="*.py" --include="*.ts"

# Or scan all flags at once:
curl -s "${UNLEASH_URL}/api/admin/archive/features" \
  -H "Authorization: ${UNLEASH_API_KEY}" | \
  jq -r '.features[].name' | while read flag; do
    count=$(grep -rn "$flag" src/ | wc -l)
    if [ "$count" -gt 0 ]; then
      echo "STALE CODE: $flag found $count times"
    fi
  done

items.title: Write a rollout runbook for your team
items.description: Progressive delivery only works if your whole team follows the same process. Write a short runbook that covers: how to create a flag, what the rollout stages are, when to pause or roll back, and who has permission to push to 100%. Keep it in your repo so it stays up to date.

codeBlocks.label: Rollout runbook template
codeBlocks.code:
# Progressive Delivery Runbook

## Rollout stages
| Stage  | Traffic | Duration | Action if errors spike |
|--------|---------|----------|------------------------|
| 1      | 1%      | 1 hour   | Roll back immediately  |
| 2      | 5%      | 4 hours  | Roll back immediately  |
| 3      | 25%     | 24 hours | Pause and investigate  |
| 4      | 50%     | 24 hours | Pause and investigate  |
| 5      | 100%    | -        | Monitor for 48 hours   |

## Roll back checklist
1. Set flag to 0% in Unleash dashboard
2. Post in #incidents Slack channel
3. Check error rate returns to baseline within 5 min
4. If errors persist, check if other flags changed recently

## When to skip stages
- Never for payment or auth changes
- Bug fixes with tests can go 1% -> 25% -> 100%
- Config-only changes can go 5% -> 100%

## Who can promote to 100%?
- Any engineer on the owning team
- Must have passed all analysis steps in Argo Rollouts
- Must have monitored for at least 24h at 50%
